refined_fr.refined_fr: Maintain teleoperation link availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold throughout the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR 'achieve the customer-defined teleoperation link availability' is vague about the temporal and comparative nature of the requirement. The refined version clarifies that availability is a sustained property (not a one-time achievement) measured against a specific threshold over a defined period. This removes ambiguity about what 'achieve' means while remaining solution-neutral—it does not prescribe redundancy, protocol choice, or network architecture. The FR is atomic: it expresses a single obligation (maintain availability above threshold) without embedded 'and'/'or' conditions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect teleoperation link uptime logs over the agreed evaluation period (e.g., 30 days of operational use). Calculate availability as (total_uptime / total_period). Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., 0.999). Pass if measured availability ≥ target.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant communication pathways with automatic failover logic to maintain continuous link availability when primary pathway degrades or fails.
DP2: Deploy real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection and proactive rerouting to alternative communication channels before availability threshold is breached.

DP3: Establish hierarchical communication architecture with local autonomous fallback mode that preserves critical teleoperation functions during link unavailability, resuming remote control upon link restoration.
DP4: Implement adaptive bandwidth management and protocol selection that dynamically adjusts communication parameters to maintain minimum viable link quality under varying network conditions.
DP5: Combine redundant pathways (DP1) with quality monitoring (DP2) and autonomous fallback (DP3) in an integrated availability assurance system with coordinated state management across modes.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Integrated availability assurance system combining (a) redundant communication pathways with automatic failover, (b) real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection, and (c) hierarchical architecture enabling local autonomous fallback during link loss, with coordinated state management across normal, degraded, and autonomous modes.

potential_sources: DP1 and DP2 are loosely coupled: failover logic (DP1) and quality monitoring (DP2) operate on independent timescales (failover is reactive; monitoring is predictive) but share common link state data—mitigated by clear interface contracts.

DP3 introduces potential coupling with DP1/DP2: autonomous fallback mode must synchronize state with remote operator upon link restoration—requires explicit handoff protocol to avoid command conflicts.
DP4 (adaptive parameters) could conflict with DP1 failover decisions if bandwidth reduction is attempted before switching pathways—mitigated by priority ordering: failover first, then adaptation.
Measurement and telemetry (implicit in DP2) must not consume link capacity needed for control—requires reserved bandwidth allocation.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 achieves high cohesion by grouping three complementary strategies (redundancy, monitoring, fallback) under a single availability objective. Coupling is manageable through explicit mode-transition protocols and bandwidth reservation. The design avoids over-specification of technology (e.g., does not mandate 4G/5G/satellite) and remains solution-neutral.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Multiple independent communication pathways are available at the worksite (e.g., cellular, WiFi, wired, satellite). RISK: If only one pathway exists, DP1 (redundancy) cannot be realized; recommend early site survey.

ASSUMPTION: Link quality metrics (latency, packet loss, signal strength) are measurable in real-time. RISK: Measurement overhead or sensor failures could degrade link availability; recommend redundant telemetry channels.

ASSUMPTION: Autonomous fallback mode (DP3) is operationally acceptable and safe under worksite safety plan. RISK: If safety plan prohibits autonomous operation, DP3 must be replaced with graceful degradation (reduced functionality) or emergency stop; recommend safety review.

ASSUMPTION: Predictive degradation detection (DP2) can be tuned to avoid false positives (unnecessary failovers) and false negatives (missed degradation). RISK: Tuning requires historical link data; if unavailable, detection may be reactive rather than predictive initially.

ASSUMPTION: State synchronization during mode transitions (normal ↔ autonomous ↔ failover) can be completed within acceptable latency bounds. RISK: If synchronization latency exceeds operator tolerance, command conflicts or loss of situational awareness may occur; recommend latency budget analysis.

ASSUMPTION: Target availability threshold (0.999 or 99.9%) is achievable with proposed DP given worksite conditions. RISK: If environmental interference or pathway reliability is poor, threshold may be unattainable; recommend baseline link characterization study.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is sufficiently long to capture representative failure modes and seasonal variations. RISK: Short evaluation windows may not expose rare failure scenarios; recommend minimum 30-day continuous operation.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) Definition of 'link availability' (e.g., round-trip latency < X ms AND packet loss < Y% OR link down), (2) Acceptable duration of autonomous fallback mode before operator intervention required, (3) Worksite communication pathway inventory and baseline quality metrics, (4) Safety constraints on autonomous operation, (5) Evaluation period duration and environmental conditions (seasonal, time-of-day variations). DP5 should be decomposed into three sub-DPs (redundancy, monitoring, fallback) with explicit mode-transition state machine and bandwidth reservation policy to enable detailed design and testing.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Integrated availability assurance system managing redundant communication pathways, link quality monitoring, and hierarchical fallback modes for teleoperated vehicle control

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface to physical/logical communication pathways; Teleoperation monitors link quality, triggers failover, and receives status of redundant pathways

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and mission directives; Teleoperation translates these into vehicle control signals, adapting transmission strategy based on link availability mode

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits safety-critical notifications when link degradation is detected, autonomous fallback is activated, or communication loss is imminent

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives real-time vehicle state and link quality metrics; used for predictive degradation detection and state coordination across normal/degraded/autonomous modes

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Teleoperation is the natural owner of communication availability assurance, link quality monitoring, and failover logic. Redundant pathways, predictive degradation detection, and mode coordination (normal/degraded/autonomous) are core to teleoperation reliability. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert notifications must propagate to Safety Services; Network Link interface is external dependency.

Moderate fit: Safety Services could own the safety-critical aspects (failover triggers, autonomous fallback validation), but would not naturally own link quality monitoring, redundant pathway management, or mode state coordination. High diffusion: core DP logic (availability assurance, predictive detection) would leak into Teleoperation. Creates split ownership and coordination overhead.

Poor fit: Vehicle module is responsible for vehicle dynamics and control execution, not communication infrastructure or teleoperation link management. Placing availability assurance here violates separation of concerns. High diffusion: Teleoperation would still need to implement failover logic, mode switching, and link monitoring independently.

Weak fit: NGHE Solution is a system-level container. While it could host the DP as a cross-cutting service, it lacks the operational context of teleoperation. Diffusion: Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Safety Services would all depend on NGHE-level availability logic, creating tight coupling and reduced autonomy. Better as a coordination layer than primary owner.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the most cohesive and least diffuse boundary for DP5. Teleoperation is inherently responsible for managing the operator-to-vehicle communication channel, making it the natural owner of redundant pathways, link quality monitoring, predictive degradation detection, and mode coordination (normal/degraded/autonomous). The DP's hierarchical architecture with local autonomous fallback aligns with Teleoperation's need to maintain control authority during link loss. State management across modes is localized within Teleoperation's control loop, minimizing external dependencies.
containment_risks: Network Link interface is external: Teleoperation depends on the availability and correctness of redundant pathway implementations. If Network Link does not expose sufficient status/control granularity, Teleoperation may struggle to implement predictive detection or failover coordination.

SafetyAlert coupling: Teleoperation must emit safety alerts to Safety Services when link degradation or autonomous fallback occurs. If Safety Services has strict latency or format requirements, Teleoperation's alert logic may need refinement.

Telemetry dependency: Real-time link quality metrics must flow from Vehicle/Network Link through Telemetry interface. Latency or data loss in Telemetry could degrade predictive degradation detection accuracy.

Autonomous fallback validation: If Safety Services must validate or override autonomous fallback decisions, Teleoperation and Safety Services require tight coordination, risking mode-switching conflicts.

State consistency across distributed modes: If Vehicle or other modules cache communication state independently, Teleoperation's coordinated state management may diverge, causing inconsistent failover behavior.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP5 is well-formulated and maps cleanly to Teleoperation. No revision needed. However, to reduce containment risk, explicitly define the contract between Teleoperation and Network Link: (1) Network Link MUST expose redundant pathway status (active/degraded/failed) in real-time; (2) Teleoperation MUST emit predictive degradation alerts via SafetyAlert before autonomous fallback; (3) Vehicle MUST NOT cache communication state—all state is owned by Teleoperation. This clarifies boundaries and reduces diffusion.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable acceptance criterion (link availability ≥ 0.999 over agreed evaluation period). The IM boundary (Teleoperation module) owns the integrated availability assurance system, making it the natural point of instrumentation for availability metrics. Real-time link quality monitoring and predictive degradation detection provide observable signals for validation. Telemetry interface supplies vehicle state and link metrics needed for measurement. Verification can be conducted through continuous monitoring during the evaluation period with clear pass/fail determination against the 0.999 threshold.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR requirement (maintain teleoperation link availability at target threshold) maps directly to the Teleoperation IM boundary, which owns redundant communication pathways, link quality monitoring, failover logic, and mode coordination. The allocation is unambiguous: Teleoperation is responsible for achieving and maintaining the availability target. External dependencies (Network Link, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) are explicitly identified and their roles in supporting the FR are well-defined.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 directly addresses the FR. The DP specifies three integrated mechanisms: (1) redundant communication pathways with automatic failover—directly supports availability maintenance; (2) real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection—enables proactive intervention before availability drops below threshold; (3) hierarchical architecture with local autonomous fallback—ensures availability is maintained even during link loss by enabling vehicle to operate autonomously. The DP is solution-complete and logically sound for achieving the stated FR. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Network Link interface dependency: Teleoperation's ability to implement predictive degradation detection and failover coordination depends on Network Link exposing real-time status of redundant pathways (active/degraded/failed). If Network Link interface lacks sufficient granularity or latency is excessive, predictive detection accuracy will degrade, risking availability target miss. Mitigation: Establish explicit contract defining Network Link status exposure requirements.

Telemetry latency and completeness: Real-time link quality metrics flow through Telemetry interface. Data loss, buffering delays, or incomplete metric transmission could degrade predictive degradation detection. If Telemetry is shared with other consumers, contention may introduce jitter. Mitigation: Define Telemetry SLA for link quality metric delivery (latency, completeness, frequency).

SafetyAlert coupling and latency: Teleoperation must emit safety-critical alerts via SafetyAlert when link degradation or autonomous fallback occurs. If Safety Services has strict latency or format requirements, or if SafetyAlert interface is congested, alerts may be delayed, reducing safety margin. Mitigation: Establish SafetyAlert latency and reliability SLA; consider direct safety-critical signaling path if needed.

Autonomous fallback validation conflict: If Safety Services must validate or override autonomous fallback decisions made by Teleoperation, mode-switching coordination becomes complex. Conflicting decisions (Teleoperation initiates fallback; Safety Services blocks it) could leave vehicle in inconsistent state. Mitigation: Pre-define fallback decision authority and validation rules; establish clear handoff protocol.

State consistency across distributed modules: If Vehicle module or other subsystems cache communication state independently (e.g., last-known operator command, link status), Teleoperation's coordinated state management may diverge, causing inconsistent failover behavior or stale command execution. Mitigation: Enforce single source of truth for communication state within Teleoperation; Vehicle must not cache communication-related state.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Teleoperation is the natural and cohesive owner of DP5. The module's core responsibility—managing operator-to-vehicle communication—aligns perfectly with the DP's integrated availability assurance logic. Redundant pathway management, link quality monitoring, predictive degradation detection, and mode coordination (normal/degraded/autonomous) are all internal to Teleoperation's control loop. The hierarchical architecture with local autonomous fallback fits Teleoperation's need to maintain control authority during link loss. Minor diffusion (0.08) is limited to external interface dependencies (Network Link, Telemetry, SafetyAlert), which are unavoidable and well-managed. No alternative module (Safety Services, Vehicle, NGHE Solution) offers better cohesion without introducing significant diffusion. Recommended action: Proceed with Teleoperation as IM owner; establish explicit interface contracts to mitigate external dependency risks.

recommended_revisions: Establish Network Link interface contract: Define that Network Link MUST expose real-time status of each redundant pathway (active/degraded/failed/unavailable) with latency ≤ 100 ms and 100% delivery reliability. Specify status update frequency (e.g., every 50 ms) to support predictive degradation detection. This reduces risk of Teleoperation failing to detect link degradation in time.

Define Telemetry SLA for link quality metrics: Specify that Telemetry MUST deliver link quality metrics (signal strength, packet loss, latency, jitter) to Teleoperation with latency ≤ 50 ms, completeness ≥ 99.9%, and frequency ≥ 10 Hz. This ensures predictive degradation detection has sufficient fidelity to meet the 0.999 availability target.

Establish SafetyAlert latency and reliability SLA: Define that SafetyAlert notifications from Teleoperation (link degradation, autonomous fallback activation, communication loss imminent) MUST be delivered to Safety Services with latency ≤ 10 ms and reliability ≥ 99.99%. This ensures safety-critical alerts reach Safety Services before autonomous fallback occurs.

Pre-define autonomous fallback decision authority: Clarify that Teleoperation has primary authority to initiate autonomous fallback when link loss is detected. If Safety Services must validate or override, establish a pre-agreed validation rule (e.g., Safety Services can block fallback only if vehicle is in unsafe state; otherwise fallback proceeds). Document decision precedence to prevent mode-switching conflicts.

Enforce single source of truth for communication state: Add explicit constraint that Vehicle module and other subsystems MUST NOT cache communication-related state (operator commands, link status, mode). All communication state is owned and managed by Teleoperation. Vehicle receives control signals from Teleoperation and executes them; it does not maintain independent communication state. This prevents state divergence during failover.

Add predictive degradation detection specification: Expand DP5 to specify the predictive degradation algorithm (e.g., trend analysis on link quality metrics, threshold-based alerts, machine learning model). Define detection latency requirement (e.g., detect degradation ≥ 5 seconds before link failure) to ensure failover can be triggered proactively. This makes the DP more concrete and verifiable.

Document hierarchical fallback modes explicitly: Clarify the three modes (normal, degraded, autonomous) with state transition rules: (1) Normal: operator has full control, link quality is good; (2) Degraded: operator has reduced control authority, Teleoperation applies transmission optimization, link quality is marginal; (3) Autonomous: operator has no control, vehicle operates autonomously, link is lost. Define transition triggers and exit conditions for each mode. This reduces ambiguity in mode coordination.

decision.justification: Gate results show FR5 passes both FR-IM and FR-DP gates with high confidence (verification feasibility: High, allocation clarity: Clear, containment: 0.92). However, the recommended_revisions list identifies seven concrete, actionable refinements that must be incorporated before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. These revisions are not optional polish—they address critical safety and architectural dependencies that directly impact the feasibility of achieving the 0.999 availability target in a safety-critical teleoperation context. Specifically: (1) Network Link interface contract is essential to ensure predictive degradation detection has sufficient signal fidelity; (2) Telemetry SLA is required to guarantee link quality metric delivery latency and completeness; (3) SafetyAlert SLA is critical for safety-critical alert propagation; (4) Autonomous fallback decision authority must be pre-defined to prevent mode-switching conflicts; (5) Single source of truth constraint prevents state divergence during failover; (6) Predictive degradation algorithm specification makes the DP concrete and verifiable; (7) Hierarchical fallback mode documentation reduces ambiguity in mode coordination. These revisions strengthen the current level (FR5, DP5, IM: Teleoperation) without requiring decomposition into sub-FRs. Once revisions are incorporated, the FR-DP-IM triad will be sufficiently detailed and risk-mitigated to support either sub-FR decomposition (if needed) or direct implementation planning.

revise.why: DP5 is logically sound but insufficiently detailed for a safety-critical system. The recommended revisions require explicit specification of: (1) Network Link interface contract (status exposure, latency, frequency); (2) Telemetry SLA (latency ≤ 50 ms, completeness ≥ 99.9%, frequency ≥ 10 Hz); (3) SafetyAlert SLA (latency ≤ 10 ms, reliability ≥ 99.99%); (4) Predictive degradation algorithm (trend analysis, threshold rules, detection latency ≥ 5 seconds before failure); (5) Hierarchical fallback mode state machine (Normal → Degraded → Autonomous with explicit transition triggers and exit conditions); (6) Autonomous fallback decision authority and validation rules (Teleoperation has primary authority; Safety Services can block only if vehicle in unsafe state); (7) Single source of truth constraint (Vehicle and other subsystems must not cache communication state). These additions transform DP5 from a high-level concept into a detailed design specification that can be verified and implemented with confidence. The revisions also establish explicit contracts with dependent modules (Network Link, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Safety Services, Vehicle), reducing diffusion risk from 0.08 to near-zero.

assumptions: Network Link module can expose real-time status of redundant pathways with latency ≤ 100 ms and 100% delivery reliability.
Telemetry interface can deliver link quality metrics (signal strength, packet loss, latency, jitter) with latency ≤ 50 ms and completeness ≥ 99.9%.
SafetyAlert interface can propagate safety-critical notifications from Teleoperation to Safety Services with latency ≤ 10 ms and reliability ≥ 99.99%.
Predictive degradation detection algorithm can identify link degradation ≥ 5 seconds before link failure, providing sufficient lead time for proactive failover.
Vehicle module can operate autonomously without caching communication state, relying entirely on Teleoperation for control signals and mode coordination.
Safety Services will accept pre-defined fallback decision authority rules and will not override Teleoperation's autonomous fallback decisions except in explicitly documented unsafe-state scenarios.
Hierarchical fallback modes (Normal, Degraded, Autonomous) can be implemented with clear state transitions and no race conditions or deadlocks during mode switching.
open_risks: Network Link interface latency or reliability shortfall: If Network Link cannot meet the ≤ 100 ms latency or 100% delivery SLA, predictive degradation detection will degrade, risking availability target miss. Mitigation: Establish Network Link SLA early; conduct latency and reliability testing before Teleoperation implementation.

Telemetry contention and jitter: If Telemetry interface is shared with other consumers (e.g., diagnostics, logging), contention may introduce variable latency or packet loss, degrading link quality metric fidelity. Mitigation: Define Telemetry SLA with priority queuing for link quality metrics; consider dedicated telemetry channel if contention is unavoidable.

SafetyAlert congestion during failover: If SafetyAlert interface is congested when Teleoperation emits multiple alerts (link degradation, autonomous fallback, communication loss imminent), alert delivery may be delayed, reducing safety margin. Mitigation: Establish SafetyAlert SLA with priority queuing; conduct stress testing with simultaneous alerts.

Autonomous fallback validation conflict: If Safety Services interprets its role as validating all Teleoperation decisions, including autonomous fallback, mode-switching coordination becomes complex and potentially deadlock-prone. Mitigation: Pre-define fallback decision authority in design review; establish clear handoff protocol with explicit decision precedence.

State divergence during failover: If Vehicle or other subsystems cache communication-related state independently (e.g., last-known operator command, link status), Teleoperation's coordinated state management may diverge during failover, causing inconsistent behavior. Mitigation: Enforce single source of truth constraint in architecture review; add runtime assertions to detect state divergence.

Predictive degradation algorithm accuracy: If the predictive degradation algorithm (trend analysis, threshold rules, or ML model) has high false-positive or false-negative rates, it may trigger unnecessary failovers or miss actual degradation. Mitigation: Specify algorithm design and validation approach; conduct field testing with representative link degradation scenarios.

Mode transition race conditions: If Normal → Degraded → Autonomous transitions are not carefully synchronized, race conditions may occur (e.g., operator sends command while mode is transitioning), leaving vehicle in inconsistent state. Mitigation: Design mode transition state machine with explicit guards and atomic transitions; add runtime monitoring for transition anomalies.
evidence_needed: Network Link interface specification: Document the real-time status API that Network Link will expose (active/degraded/failed/unavailable for each redundant pathway), latency guarantee (≤ 100 ms), delivery reliability (100%), and update frequency (e.g., every 50 ms). Conduct latency and reliability testing to verify compliance.

Telemetry SLA agreement: Define the link quality metrics (signal strength, packet loss, latency, jitter) that Telemetry will deliver to Teleoperation, with latency ≤ 50 ms, completeness ≥ 99.9%, and frequency ≥ 10 Hz. Conduct end-to-end latency testing and packet loss analysis.

SafetyAlert SLA agreement: Define the alert notification format, latency guarantee (≤ 10 ms), and reliability (≥ 99.99%) for safety-critical alerts from Teleoperation to Safety Services. Conduct stress testing with simultaneous alerts to verify SLA compliance.

Predictive degradation algorithm specification: Document the algorithm (trend analysis, threshold rules, ML model, or hybrid approach), detection latency requirement (≥ 5 seconds before link failure), and validation approach (simulation, field testing, or both). Provide algorithm design rationale and sensitivity analysis.

Hierarchical fallback mode state machine: Document the three modes (Normal, Degraded, Autonomous) with explicit state transition rules, triggers, and exit conditions. Include timing constraints (e.g., transition latency ≤ 100 ms) and race condition analysis.

Autonomous fallback decision authority agreement: Document the decision authority rules (Teleoperation has primary authority; Safety Services can block only if vehicle in unsafe state) and obtain sign-off from Safety Services and Vehicle teams. Include conflict resolution protocol.

Single source of truth constraint enforcement: Document the constraint that Vehicle and other subsystems must not cache communication-related state. Add runtime assertions or monitoring to detect state divergence. Conduct integration testing to verify constraint compliance.

Safety-critical alert propagation testing: Conduct end-to-end testing of SafetyAlert notifications during simulated link degradation and autonomous fallback scenarios. Verify that alerts reach Safety Services with required latency and reliability.
